title: Chemical Eye Injury
oneLiner: An ophthalmologic emergency from alkali (worse) or acid exposure causing corneal and conjunctival necrosis, with high risk of blindness if irrigation is delayed.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
**Classic vignette:**  
A 34-year-old male presents to the ED 20 minutes after a splash of drain cleaner to his right eye at work. He is in severe pain, photophobic, and unable to open his eye. On exam, the conjunctiva is chemotic and white (whitened), the cornea has a hazy, ground-glass appearance, and you can barely see the iris. The anterior chamber appears shallow. The pH of the inferior fornix is 11.0.

**Dissect the vignette – pattern clues:**

- **Drain cleaner** → alkali (sodium hydroxide, KOH or NaOH) → deep penetration, worse outcome
- **Severe pain, photophobia** → corneal epithelial and nerve damage; trigeminal irritation
- **Chemotic, white conjunctiva** → ischemia and blanching of vessels from vasoconstriction/necrosis
- **Ground-glass cornea** → stromal edema and haze from collagen swelling; loss of clarity
- **Shallow anterior chamber** → possible scleral perforation or severe intraocular pressure rise
- **pH 11.0** → confirms alkali; pH >9 is high-risk for limbal stem cell loss

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**

- **Delayed presentation** (hours later): Pain may have subsided due to nerve destruction, but eye is still red – the “silent” injury. Patient may underreport severity.
- **Low-grade chemical** (e.g., shampoo, cement dust): Mild irritation, normal vision initially but may develop conjunctival scarring weeks later (cicatricial entropion).
- **Pseudo-alkali injury from solvents** (e.g., acetone): Immediate pain and tearing but pH normal; actually a solvent toxicity causing epithelial sloughing, not chemical burn.
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
**Plain-language definition:**  
Chemical eye injury is damage to the ocular surface (cornea, conjunctiva) and deeper structures caused by exposure to a corrosive chemical, most commonly alkali or acid. The injury ranges from mild epithelial defect to full-thickness corneal necrosis with limbal ischemia.

**Why it matters clinically:**

- **Frequency:** One of the most common ocular emergencies; about 20% of all eye injuries in industrial settings. Kids also – household cleaners, laundry pods.
- **Stakes:** Alkali injuries can cause irreversible blindness within hours if not irrigated. Even “minor” burns can lead to limbal stem cell deficiency, corneal scarring, glaucoma, and phthisis.
- **Common pitfalls:** Not checking pH after irrigation, under-treating pain, missing limbal ischemia (grade II+), and forgetting long-term glaucoma monitoring.
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The \"Why\" Behind:
**The story of injury:**

- **Contact:** Chemical splashes onto cornea and conjunctiva. The eye’s natural tear film and blinking provide some dilution, but concentrated chemicals overwhelm.
- **pH-driven damage:**  
  - **Alkali** (high pH) saponifies fatty acids in cell membranes → rapid cell death. Also penetrates the stroma because hydroxide ions break collagen cross-links and cause “liquefactive necrosis”. Can enter the anterior chamber within minutes → damage to iris, ciliary body, lens.  
  - **Acid** (low pH) causes protein coagulation (precipitate) which forms a barrier that limits penetration → damage is more superficial.
- **Limbal ischemia:** Chemical damages the limbal vasculature → occlusion of vessels → loss of blood supply to the cornea → stem cell death. White conjunctiva = ischemia.
- **Corneal haze:** Stromal edema from osmotically active molecules and disrupted endothelial pump → ground-glass appearance.
- **Inflammatory cascade:** Release of prostaglandins, cytokines → secondary inflammation, neovascularization, scarring.

**Mechanism → sign:**

| Mechanism | Sign |
|-----------|------|
| Lipid saponification (alkali) | Rapid, deep penetration; pain out of proportion to visible injury early |
| Collagen denaturation (alkali) | Corneal haze, stromal necrosis, blistering |
| Limbal vessel occlusion | White (blanched) conjunctiva – “the limbal whitening sign” |
| Epithelial defect | Fluorescein staining, pain, photophobia |
| Endothelial pump failure (anterior segment) | Corneal edema, decreased vision |
| Ciliary body damage | Hypotony (low IOP) initially; later glaucoma from scarring |

**Connect to pattern recognition:**  
The vignette’s white conjunctiva and ground-glass cornea are direct outcomes of limbal ischemia and stromal edema. The pH 11 is the driver — alkali.
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins):
| Category | Examples | Stem clue (how they present) |
|----------|----------|------------------------------|
| **Alkali** (most common and severe) | Sodium/potassium hydroxide (drain cleaner, oven cleaner, soap, cement), Ammonia (household cleaners, fertilizer), Lime (plaster, mortar) | “Splash of drain cleaner / cement powder / household cleaner in eye.” |
| **Acid** (less severe) | Sulfuric (battery acid), Hydrochloric (pool chemicals), Hydrofluoric (industrial glass etching) – HF is **not** like other acids; it causes deep penetration and systemic hypocalcemia | “Battery acid splash” or “pool acid”; HF gives burning pain and systemic symptoms. |
| **Solvents** | Acetone (nail polish remover), alcohol, paint thinner | “Pain and tearing but pH normal”; epithelial sloughing but limited stromal damage. |
| **Surfactants/Detergents** | Dishwasher pods, laundry liquid | “Child with bubble in eye”; mild epithelial injury, no stromal damage. |
| **Other** | Pepper spray (oleoresin capsicum), Mace, CS gas | Severe tearing and blepharospasm but pH normal; pain is from irritation, not burn. |

**Most common cause worldwide:** Alkali (specifically sodium hydroxide from household cleaners). In developing countries, **lime (calcium oxide)** from cement is a huge problem.
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic scoring:**  
**Roper-Hall (Dua) classification** – grade based on limbal ischemia and corneal opacity.  
- Grade I: limbal ischemia 0%, clear cornea – excellent prognosis.
- Grade II: up to 1/3 limbal ischemia, hazy but visible iris – good.
- Grade III: 1/3 to 1/2 limbal ischemia, corneal opacity obscures iris – guarded.
- Grade IV: >50% limbal ischemia, corneal opacity total – poor, high risk of blindness.

**Key tests:**

| Test | What it shows | Why order | Interpretation |
|------|---------------|-----------|----------------|
| **pH testing** (litmus paper in inferior fornix) | Chemical nature and residual alkalinity/acidity | Initial – to confirm exposure and guide irrigation endpoint | Normal = 7.4. Aim for 7.0–8.0 after irrigation. Higher or lower means ongoing damage. |
| **Slit-lamp exam with fluorescein** | Epithelial defect (stains green) | Assess corneal surface integrity; size of defect correlates with severity | Widespread staining = high grade. Central defect with heaped edges = limbal stem cell loss. |
| **Check intraocular pressure (IOP)** | Elevated IOP due to scleral edema or secondary glaucoma | In all moderate/severe burns; high IOP can cause optic neuropathy | >30 mmHg needs immediate treatment. |
| **Anterior chamber exam** | Hyphema, shallow chamber, iritis | Detect penetration | Hyphema + shallow AC = possible scleral perforation or severe anterior necrosis. |
| **Lactate in severe alkali (if HF)** | Systemic hypocalcemia in HF burns | Only for hydrofluoric acid exposure – systemic toxicity can be fatal | Calcium levels, ECG (QTc prolongation). |

**Single finding that confirms severity:** **>50% limbal ischemia (white conjunctiva)** = Grade IV burn, highest risk of blindness.

**Single finding that rules out serious injury:** **Normal pH (<8.0 and >6.5) after irrigation, no epithelial defect, and no limbal ischemia.**
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
**Immediate (first hour):**

1. **Stop exposure** – remove contact lenses if present.
2. **Irrigate immediately** – the single most critical intervention. Use **0.9% saline** or **Ringer’s lactate** – tap water if nothing else.  
   - **Volume:** 1–2 litres per eye *minimum*; continue until pH normalises (test every 5 min).  
   - **Technique:** A Morgan lens or IV tubing directed into the fornices. Evert eyelids to wash away particles.  
   - **Duration:** At least 15–30 minutes for alkali; longer for undiluted solids (cement).
3. **Pain control** – topical anaesthetic (proxymetacaine 0.5% or tetracaine 0.5% – **only one drop initially** to facilitate exam; then oral analgesia (ibuprofen 400 mg PO or morphine IV if severe). Avoid repeated topical anaesthetic – delays healing.
4. **pH check** – after irrigation, confirm pH 7–8. If still high, continue irrigation.
5. **Debridement** – remove necrotic epithelium and any retained particles with moistened swab.

**Ward (24–72 hours):**

- **Topical antibiotics:** Chloramphenicol 0.5% drops QDS or ofloxacin 0.3% QDS – to prevent secondary infection.
- **Topical steroids:** Dexamethasone 0.1% drops 4–6 hourly – to reduce inflammation and prevent scarring. **But:** In limbal ischemia >50% (Grade IV), steroids may worsen healing – consult ophthalmology.
- **Ascorbate (vitamin C):** 500 mg PO BD – promotes collagen synthesis; shown to reduce corneal perforation in alkali burns.
- **Cycloplegic:** Cyclopentolate 1% TDS – relieves ciliary spasm pain and prevents posterior synechiae.
- **IOP management:** If IOP >30 mmHg – add timolol 0.5% BD + oral acetazolamide 250 mg QDS (IV if unable to take PO). Monitor IOP daily.
- **Topical citrate?** Controversial; can reduce inflammatory mediators but not standard.

**What NOT to do:**

- **Don’t** use topical anaesthetic repeatedly – the patient will feel no pain but healing stops, leading to neurotrophic ulcer.
- **Don’t** patch the eye – increases risk of infection and delays epithelial healing.
- **Don’t** use topical NSAIDs (e.g., diclofenac) – can cause corneal melt.
- **Don’t** forget to remove any solid particles in the fornices (e.g., cement clumps).
- **Don’t** discharge without ophthalmology review – even mild burns can develop late scarring.

**Long-term (weeks to months):**

- **Amniotic membrane transplant** – for persistent epithelial defects or limbal stem cell deficiency.  
- **Limbal stem cell transplantation** – if bilateral severe burns.  
- **Glaucoma management** – may need trabeculectomy or tube shunt if scarring impairs drainage.  
- **Lubricants** – preservative-free artificial tears QDS to maintain ocular surface.  
- **Scleral contact lens** – for corneal irregularity and protection.
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | Chemical Eye Injury | Corneal Abrasion | Acute Angle-Closure Glaucoma | Conjunctivitis |
|---------|---------------------|------------------|------------------------------|----------------|
| Pain | Severe, immediate, burning | Sharp, foreign-body sensation | Deep, throbbing (often headache) | Gritty, mild |
| Redness | Diffuse conjunctival injection + limbal whitening | Localized injection near defect | Ciliary flush (circumcorneal) | Diffuse, not periorbital |
| Vision | Decreased often out of proportion to lesion | Decreased if central defect | Blurred, halos around lights | Normal or mildly blurred |
| Corneal clarity | Hazy (ground-glass) | Clear except for defect | Cloudy (epithelial edema) | Clear |
| Pupil | Normal or mid-dilated (if high IOP) | Normal | Mid-dilated, fixed, oval | Normal |
| IOP | Variable – can be low (ciliary damage) or high (edema) | Normal | Very high (40–80 mmHg) | Normal |
| Key distinguishing feature | History of chemical exposure + abnormal pH | No history of chemical; linear foreign body sensation often | Mid-dilated fixed pupil with shallow anterior chamber | No corneal haze; both eyes often affected |

**How to quickly differentiate:**  
- pH test is the **specific** differential – if normal, think other causes.  
- Limbal whitening = chemical injury until proven otherwise.  
- Shallow anterior chamber + fixed mid-dilated pupil = angle closure, not chemical.
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
**Major complications with time course:**

- **Acute (hours–24h):** Persistent low IOP (hypotony) from ciliary body necrosis – presents with soft eye, decreased vision. **Action:** Urgent ophthalmology likely needs topical steroids and cycloplegics; can later lead to phthisis.
- **Subacute (days–2 weeks):**  
  - **Corneal ulceration/perforation** – due to non-healing epithelial defect. Signs: sudden worsening pain, positive Seidel test (fluorescein streaming with aqueous). **Action:** Emergent cyanoacrylate glue or patch graft.
  - **Secondary glaucoma** – from trabecular meshwork inflammation or scarring. Presents with rising IOP, pain, corneal edema. **Action:** Topical antiglaucoma drops; if refractory, surgical drainage.
- **Chronic (weeks–months):**  
  - **Limbal stem cell deficiency** – leads to recurrent epithelial breakdown, conjunctivalisation of cornea (fibrovascular ingrowth). Signs: persistent defect, opacity, neovascularization. **Action:** Limbal transplantation.
  - **Cicatricial entropion** – conjunctival scarring pulls eyelid margin inward → trichiasis (lashes rubbing cornea). **Action:** Eyelid surgery.
  - **Phthisis bulbi** – end-stage shrunken blind eye from chronic inflammation and hypotony. **Action:** Enucleation if painful.

**Late-presentation patterns (exam stems):**

- “A patient who had a chemical eye injury 2 months ago now presents with decreased vision, photophobia, and a patch of vascularised scar on the superior cornea” → **limbal stem cell deficiency**.
- “A patient with previous alkali burn now has a chronically red eye, elevated IOP, and optic disc cupping” → **secondary glaucoma**.
- “The eye is soft, the cornea is opaque, and there is no light perception” → **phthisis bulbi**.

**What to do if untreated:** Progression from Grade I/II (good outlook) to Grade IV (blindness). The “window period” for intervention is hours for irrigation; weeks for steroids and wound-healing support.
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonics:**

1. **C-O-R-N-E-A** for immediate management steps:  
   - C – Chemical removal (irrigate, remove particles)  
   - O – pH check (before and after irrigation)  
   - R – Remove necrotic epithelium  
   - N – Never use topical anaesthetic repeatedly  
   - E – Eye exam (slit lamp, fluorescein, IOP, limbal ischemia)  
   - A – Antibiotic, steroids, cycloplegic, ascorbate

2. **A-L-K-A-L-I** for severity grading signs:  
   - A – Anterior chamber abnormalities  
   - L – Limbal ischemia (white conjunctiva) – the key predictor  
   - K – Corneal hazing (ground-glass)  
   - A – Area of epithelial defect (assess with fluorescein)  
   - L – Light sensitivity (photophobia)  
   - I – Intraocular pressure

**Clinical pearls (pattern-recognition cues):**

1. **“White is bad”** – the whiter the conjunctiva (limbal ischemia), the worse the prognosis. Pink or red conjunctiva indicates preservation of blood supply.
2. **pH does not tell the whole story** – a patient with pH 8 after irrigation may still have significant limbal ischemia if the chemical was alkali. Always examine limbal vasculature.
3. **If the patient has no pain, be suspicious** – severe alkali burns can destroy corneal nerves, leading to a painless injury that is actually very deep.
4. **Don’t forget the eyelids** – evert upper lid to look for particles; missed cement clumps can cause ongoing damage.
5. **Hydrofluoric acid is a hidden killer** – it penetrates deeply and chelates calcium causing hypocalcemia; check serum calcium and ECG, and use topical calcium gluconate gel.
6. **Topical steroids are a double-edged sword** – they reduce inflammation but delay epithelial healing. Only use for moderate burns (Grade I–II) under ophthalmology guidance; withhold if there is >50% limbal ischemia.
7. **Ascorbate is the forgotten drug** – oral vitamin C 500 mg BD has shown to reduce perforation risk in alkali burns. Start early.

**Common exam/ward mistakes to avoid:**

- **Not checking pH after irrigation** – you might stop too early.
- **Using topical anaesthetic for pain control** – leads to neurotrophic keratopathy; use oral analgesia.
- **Patching the eye** – increases infection risk; leave open or use a shield.
- **Discharging a mild chemical burn** – even grade I can develop late scarring; always refer to ophthalmology clinic for follow-up at 1–2 weeks.
- **Forgetting intraocular pressure assessment** – patients can lose vision from glaucoma without noticeable symptoms.
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
**When to call senior / ophthalmology immediately:**

- **Any limbal ischemia >50%** (Grade III or IV) – need urgent ophthalmology assessment for possible amnion or surgery.
- **Corneal perforation** (Seidel test positive) – emergency patch graft.
- **Raised IOP >40 mmHg** despite topical therapy – may need IV acetazolamide or surgical intervention.
- **Hypotony** (IOP <6 mmHg) with shallow anterior chamber – suspect ciliary body necrosis or penetration.
- **Hydrofluoric acid exposure** – even if eye looks mild, check serum calcium. If hypocalcemia (corrected Ca <2.1) or ECG shows QTc prolongation, call ICU for IV calcium gluconate.
- **Bilateral severe burns** (both eyes) – need senior ophthalmology and consider ICU for sedation to allow ongoing irrigation.
- **Any patient with decreased level of consciousness** (GCS <15) or inability to open eyes – may have ingested chemical; assess airway, need ICU.

**Specific thresholds (NEWS2 / standard vitals):**

- NEWS2 ≥5 – escalate to medical team and ophthalmology.
- **Lactate** – not routinely needed unless HF exposure. If >2 mmol/L in HF, high risk systemic toxicity.
- **GCS <15** – airway and C-spine if mechanism suggests trauma (falls, fights). Call crash team if GCS <8.
- **IOP >50 mmHg** – immediate emergency; call ophthalmology senior and arrange paracentesis if drops fail.

**When to call crash team / ICU:**

- **Cardiac arrest** from hypocalcemia (HF) or vagal reaction to pain.
- **Seizure** from hypocalcemia.
- **Anaphylaxis** to topical anaesthetic (rare).
- **Inhalation injury** with stridor or hypoxia – chemical fumes (ammonia, chlorine) can cause airway edema.

rawMarkdown:
## Chemical Eye Injury

**One-liner:** An ophthalmologic emergency from alkali (worse) or acid exposure causing corneal and conjunctival necrosis, with high risk of blindness if irrigation is delayed.

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See

**Classic vignette:**  
A 34-year-old male presents to the ED 20 minutes after a splash of drain cleaner to his right eye at work. He is in severe pain, photophobic, and unable to open his eye. On exam, the conjunctiva is chemotic and white (whitened), the cornea has a hazy, ground-glass appearance, and you can barely see the iris. The anterior chamber appears shallow. The pH of the inferior fornix is 11.0.

**Dissect the vignette – pattern clues:**

- **Drain cleaner** → alkali (sodium hydroxide, KOH or NaOH) → deep penetration, worse outcome
- **Severe pain, photophobia** → corneal epithelial and nerve damage; trigeminal irritation
- **Chemotic, white conjunctiva** → ischemia and blanching of vessels from vasoconstriction/necrosis
- **Ground-glass cornea** → stromal edema and haze from collagen swelling; loss of clarity
- **Shallow anterior chamber** → possible scleral perforation or severe intraocular pressure rise
- **pH 11.0** → confirms alkali; pH >9 is high-risk for limbal stem cell loss

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**

- **Delayed presentation** (hours later): Pain may have subsided due to nerve destruction, but eye is still red – the “silent” injury. Patient may underreport severity.
- **Low-grade chemical** (e.g., shampoo, cement dust): Mild irritation, normal vision initially but may develop conjunctival scarring weeks later (cicatricial entropion).
- **Pseudo-alkali injury from solvents** (e.g., acetone): Immediate pain and tearing but pH normal; actually a solvent toxicity causing epithelial sloughing, not chemical burn.

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)

**Plain-language definition:**  
Chemical eye injury is damage to the ocular surface (cornea, conjunctiva) and deeper structures caused by exposure to a corrosive chemical, most commonly alkali or acid. The injury ranges from mild epithelial defect to full-thickness corneal necrosis with limbal ischemia.

**Why it matters clinically:**

- **Frequency:** One of the most common ocular emergencies; about 20% of all eye injuries in industrial settings. Kids also – household cleaners, laundry pods.
- **Stakes:** Alkali injuries can cause irreversible blindness within hours if not irrigated. Even “minor” burns can lead to limbal stem cell deficiency, corneal scarring, glaucoma, and phthisis.
- **Common pitfalls:** Not checking pH after irrigation, under-treating pain, missing limbal ischemia (grade II+), and forgetting long-term glaucoma monitoring.

### 3. Pathophysiology — The "Why" Behind The Findings

**The story of injury:**

- **Contact:** Chemical splashes onto cornea and conjunctiva. The eye’s natural tear film and blinking provide some dilution, but concentrated chemicals overwhelm.
- **pH-driven damage:**  
  - **Alkali** (high pH) saponifies fatty acids in cell membranes → rapid cell death. Also penetrates the stroma because hydroxide ions break collagen cross-links and cause “liquefactive necrosis”. Can enter the anterior chamber within minutes → damage to iris, ciliary body, lens.  
  - **Acid** (low pH) causes protein coagulation (precipitate) which forms a barrier that limits penetration → damage is more superficial.
- **Limbal ischemia:** Chemical damages the limbal vasculature → occlusion of vessels → loss of blood supply to the cornea → stem cell death. White conjunctiva = ischemia.
- **Corneal haze:** Stromal edema from osmotically active molecules and disrupted endothelial pump → ground-glass appearance.
- **Inflammatory cascade:** Release of prostaglandins, cytokines → secondary inflammation, neovascularization, scarring.

**Mechanism → sign:**

| Mechanism | Sign |
|-----------|------|
| Lipid saponification (alkali) | Rapid, deep penetration; pain out of proportion to visible injury early |
| Collagen denaturation (alkali) | Corneal haze, stromal necrosis, blistering |
| Limbal vessel occlusion | White (blanched) conjunctiva – “the limbal whitening sign” |
| Epithelial defect | Fluorescein staining, pain, photophobia |
| Endothelial pump failure (anterior segment) | Corneal edema, decreased vision |
| Ciliary body damage | Hypotony (low IOP) initially; later glaucoma from scarring |

**Connect to pattern recognition:**  
The vignette’s white conjunctiva and ground-glass cornea are direct outcomes of limbal ischemia and stromal edema. The pH 11 is the driver — alkali.

### 4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins)

| Category | Examples | Stem clue (how they present) |
|----------|----------|------------------------------|
| **Alkali** (most common and severe) | Sodium/potassium hydroxide (drain cleaner, oven cleaner, soap, cement), Ammonia (household cleaners, fertilizer), Lime (plaster, mortar) | “Splash of drain cleaner / cement powder / household cleaner in eye.” |
| **Acid** (less severe) | Sulfuric (battery acid), Hydrochloric (pool chemicals), Hydrofluoric (industrial glass etching) – HF is **not** like other acids; it causes deep penetration and systemic hypocalcemia | “Battery acid splash” or “pool acid”; HF gives burning pain and systemic symptoms. |
| **Solvents** | Acetone (nail polish remover), alcohol, paint thinner | “Pain and tearing but pH normal”; epithelial sloughing but limited stromal damage. |
| **Surfactants/Detergents** | Dishwasher pods, laundry liquid | “Child with bubble in eye”; mild epithelial injury, no stromal damage. |
| **Other** | Pepper spray (oleoresin capsicum), Mace, CS gas | Severe tearing and blepharospasm but pH normal; pain is from irritation, not burn. |

**Most common cause worldwide:** Alkali (specifically sodium hydroxide from household cleaners). In developing countries, **lime (calcium oxide)** from cement is a huge problem.

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means

**Diagnostic scoring:**  
**Roper-Hall (Dua) classification** – grade based on limbal ischemia and corneal opacity.  
- Grade I: limbal ischemia 0%, clear cornea – excellent prognosis.
- Grade II: up to 1/3 limbal ischemia, hazy but visible iris – good.
- Grade III: 1/3 to 1/2 limbal ischemia, corneal opacity obscures iris – guarded.
- Grade IV: >50% limbal ischemia, corneal opacity total – poor, high risk of blindness.

**Key tests:**

| Test | What it shows | Why order | Interpretation |
|------|---------------|-----------|----------------|
| **pH testing** (litmus paper in inferior fornix) | Chemical nature and residual alkalinity/acidity | Initial – to confirm exposure and guide irrigation endpoint | Normal = 7.4. Aim for 7.0–8.0 after irrigation. Higher or lower means ongoing damage. |
| **Slit-lamp exam with fluorescein** | Epithelial defect (stains green) | Assess corneal surface integrity; size of defect correlates with severity | Widespread staining = high grade. Central defect with heaped edges = limbal stem cell loss. |
| **Check intraocular pressure (IOP)** | Elevated IOP due to scleral edema or secondary glaucoma | In all moderate/severe burns; high IOP can cause optic neuropathy | >30 mmHg needs immediate treatment. |
| **Anterior chamber exam** | Hyphema, shallow chamber, iritis | Detect penetration | Hyphema + shallow AC = possible scleral perforation or severe anterior necrosis. |
| **Lactate in severe alkali (if HF)** | Systemic hypocalcemia in HF burns | Only for hydrofluoric acid exposure – systemic toxicity can be fatal | Calcium levels, ECG (QTc prolongation). |

**Single finding that confirms severity:** **>50% limbal ischemia (white conjunctiva)** = Grade IV burn, highest risk of blindness.

**Single finding that rules out serious injury:** **Normal pH (<8.0 and >6.5) after irrigation, no epithelial defect, and no limbal ischemia.**

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning

**Immediate (first hour):**

1. **Stop exposure** – remove contact lenses if present.
2. **Irrigate immediately** – the single most critical intervention. Use **0.9% saline** or **Ringer’s lactate** – tap water if nothing else.  
   - **Volume:** 1–2 litres per eye *minimum*; continue until pH normalises (test every 5 min).  
   - **Technique:** A Morgan lens or IV tubing directed into the fornices. Evert eyelids to wash away particles.  
   - **Duration:** At least 15–30 minutes for alkali; longer for undiluted solids (cement).
3. **Pain control** – topical anaesthetic (proxymetacaine 0.5% or tetracaine 0.5% – **only one drop initially** to facilitate exam; then oral analgesia (ibuprofen 400 mg PO or morphine IV if severe). Avoid repeated topical anaesthetic – delays healing.
4. **pH check** – after irrigation, confirm pH 7–8. If still high, continue irrigation.
5. **Debridement** – remove necrotic epithelium and any retained particles with moistened swab.

**Ward (24–72 hours):**

- **Topical antibiotics:** Chloramphenicol 0.5% drops QDS or ofloxacin 0.3% QDS – to prevent secondary infection.
- **Topical steroids:** Dexamethasone 0.1% drops 4–6 hourly – to reduce inflammation and prevent scarring. **But:** In limbal ischemia >50% (Grade IV), steroids may worsen healing – consult ophthalmology.
- **Ascorbate (vitamin C):** 500 mg PO BD – promotes collagen synthesis; shown to reduce corneal perforation in alkali burns.
- **Cycloplegic:** Cyclopentolate 1% TDS – relieves ciliary spasm pain and prevents posterior synechiae.
- **IOP management:** If IOP >30 mmHg – add timolol 0.5% BD + oral acetazolamide 250 mg QDS (IV if unable to take PO). Monitor IOP daily.
- **Topical citrate?** Controversial; can reduce inflammatory mediators but not standard.

**What NOT to do:**

- **Don’t** use topical anaesthetic repeatedly – the patient will feel no pain but healing stops, leading to neurotrophic ulcer.
- **Don’t** patch the eye – increases risk of infection and delays epithelial healing.
- **Don’t** use topical NSAIDs (e.g., diclofenac) – can cause corneal melt.
- **Don’t** forget to remove any solid particles in the fornices (e.g., cement clumps).
- **Don’t** discharge without ophthalmology review – even mild burns can develop late scarring.

**Long-term (weeks to months):**

- **Amniotic membrane transplant** – for persistent epithelial defects or limbal stem cell deficiency.  
- **Limbal stem cell transplantation** – if bilateral severe burns.  
- **Glaucoma management** – may need trabeculectomy or tube shunt if scarring impairs drainage.  
- **Lubricants** – preservative-free artificial tears QDS to maintain ocular surface.  
- **Scleral contact lens** – for corneal irregularity and protection.  

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

| Feature | Chemical Eye Injury | Corneal Abrasion | Acute Angle-Closure Glaucoma | Conjunctivitis |
|---------|---------------------|------------------|------------------------------|----------------|
| Pain | Severe, immediate, burning | Sharp, foreign-body sensation | Deep, throbbing (often headache) | Gritty, mild |
| Redness | Diffuse conjunctival injection + limbal whitening | Localized injection near defect | Ciliary flush (circumcorneal) | Diffuse, not periorbital |
| Vision | Decreased often out of proportion to lesion | Decreased if central defect | Blurred, halos around lights | Normal or mildly blurred |
| Corneal clarity | Hazy (ground-glass) | Clear except for defect | Cloudy (epithelial edema) | Clear |
| Pupil | Normal or mid-dilated (if high IOP) | Normal | Mid-dilated, fixed, oval | Normal |
| IOP | Variable – can be low (ciliary damage) or high (edema) | Normal | Very high (40–80 mmHg) | Normal |
| Key distinguishing feature | History of chemical exposure + abnormal pH | No history of chemical; linear foreign body sensation often | Mid-dilated fixed pupil with shallow anterior chamber | No corneal haze; both eyes often affected |

**How to quickly differentiate:**  
- pH test is the **specific** differential – if normal, think other causes.  
- Limbal whitening = chemical injury until proven otherwise.  
- Shallow anterior chamber + fixed mid-dilated pupil = angle closure, not chemical.

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?

**Major complications with time course:**

- **Acute (hours–24h):** Persistent low IOP (hypotony) from ciliary body necrosis – presents with soft eye, decreased vision. **Action:** Urgent ophthalmology likely needs topical steroids and cycloplegics; can later lead to phthisis.
- **Subacute (days–2 weeks):**  
  - **Corneal ulceration/perforation** – due to non-healing epithelial defect. Signs: sudden worsening pain, positive Seidel test (fluorescein streaming with aqueous). **Action:** Emergent cyanoacrylate glue or patch graft.
  - **Secondary glaucoma** – from trabecular meshwork inflammation or scarring. Presents with rising IOP, pain, corneal edema. **Action:** Topical antiglaucoma drops; if refractory, surgical drainage.
- **Chronic (weeks–months):**  
  - **Limbal stem cell deficiency** – leads to recurrent epithelial breakdown, conjunctivalisation of cornea (fibrovascular ingrowth). Signs: persistent defect, opacity, neovascularization. **Action:** Limbal transplantation.
  - **Cicatricial entropion** – conjunctival scarring pulls eyelid margin inward → trichiasis (lashes rubbing cornea). **Action:** Eyelid surgery.
  - **Phthisis bulbi** – end-stage shrunken blind eye from chronic inflammation and hypotony. **Action:** Enucleation if painful.

**Late-presentation patterns (exam stems):**

- “A patient who had a chemical eye injury 2 months ago now presents with decreased vision, photophobia, and a patch of vascularised scar on the superior cornea” → **limbal stem cell deficiency**.
- “A patient with previous alkali burn now has a chronically red eye, elevated IOP, and optic disc cupping” → **secondary glaucoma**.
- “The eye is soft, the cornea is opaque, and there is no light perception” → **phthisis bulbi**.

**What to do if untreated:** Progression from Grade I/II (good outlook) to Grade IV (blindness). The “window period” for intervention is hours for irrigation; weeks for steroids and wound-healing support.

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls

**Mnemonics:**

1. **C-O-R-N-E-A** for immediate management steps:  
   - C – Chemical removal (irrigate, remove particles)  
   - O – pH check (before and after irrigation)  
   - R – Remove necrotic epithelium  
   - N – Never use topical anaesthetic repeatedly  
   - E – Eye exam (slit lamp, fluorescein, IOP, limbal ischemia)  
   - A – Antibiotic, steroids, cycloplegic, ascorbate

2. **A-L-K-A-L-I** for severity grading signs:  
   - A – Anterior chamber abnormalities  
   - L – Limbal ischemia (white conjunctiva) – the key predictor  
   - K – Corneal hazing (ground-glass)  
   - A – Area of epithelial defect (assess with fluorescein)  
   - L – Light sensitivity (photophobia)  
   - I – Intraocular pressure

**Clinical pearls (pattern-recognition cues):**

1. **“White is bad”** – the whiter the conjunctiva (limbal ischemia), the worse the prognosis. Pink or red conjunctiva indicates preservation of blood supply.
2. **pH does not tell the whole story** – a patient with pH 8 after irrigation may still have significant limbal ischemia if the chemical was alkali. Always examine limbal vasculature.
3. **If the patient has no pain, be suspicious** – severe alkali burns can destroy corneal nerves, leading to a painless injury that is actually very deep.
4. **Don’t forget the eyelids** – evert upper lid to look for particles; missed cement clumps can cause ongoing damage.
5. **Hydrofluoric acid is a hidden killer** – it penetrates deeply and chelates calcium causing hypocalcemia; check serum calcium and ECG, and use topical calcium gluconate gel.
6. **Topical steroids are a double-edged sword** – they reduce inflammation but delay epithelial healing. Only use for moderate burns (Grade I–II) under ophthalmology guidance; withhold if there is >50% limbal ischemia.
7. **Ascorbate is the forgotten drug** – oral vitamin C 500 mg BD has shown to reduce perforation risk in alkali burns. Start early.

**Common exam/ward mistakes to avoid:**

- **Not checking pH after irrigation** – you might stop too early.
- **Using topical anaesthetic for pain control** – leads to neurotrophic keratopathy; use oral analgesia.
- **Patching the eye** – increases infection risk; leave open or use a shield.
- **Discharging a mild chemical burn** – even grade I can develop late scarring; always refer to ophthalmology clinic for follow-up at 1–2 weeks.
- **Forgetting intraocular pressure assessment** – patients can lose vision from glaucoma without noticeable symptoms.

### 10. Escalation Criteria

**When to call senior / ophthalmology immediately:**

- **Any limbal ischemia >50%** (Grade III or IV) – need urgent ophthalmology assessment for possible amnion or surgery.
- **Corneal perforation** (Seidel test positive) – emergency patch graft.
- **Raised IOP >40 mmHg** despite topical therapy – may need IV acetazolamide or surgical intervention.
- **Hypotony** (IOP <6 mmHg) with shallow anterior chamber – suspect ciliary body necrosis or penetration.
- **Hydrofluoric acid exposure** – even if eye looks mild, check serum calcium. If hypocalcemia (corrected Ca <2.1) or ECG shows QTc prolongation, call ICU for IV calcium gluconate.
- **Bilateral severe burns** (both eyes) – need senior ophthalmology and consider ICU for sedation to allow ongoing irrigation.
- **Any patient with decreased level of consciousness** (GCS <15) or inability to open eyes – may have ingested chemical; assess airway, need ICU.

**Specific thresholds (NEWS2 / standard vitals):**

- NEWS2 ≥5 – escalate to medical team and ophthalmology.
- **Lactate** – not routinely needed unless HF exposure. If >2 mmol/L in HF, high risk systemic toxicity.
- **GCS <15** – airway and C-spine if mechanism suggests trauma (falls, fights). Call crash team if GCS <8.
- **IOP >50 mmHg** – immediate emergency; call ophthalmology senior and arrange paracentesis if drops fail.

**When to call crash team / ICU:**

- **Cardiac arrest** from hypocalcemia (HF) or vagal reaction to pain.
- **Seizure** from hypocalcemia.
- **Anaphylaxis** to topical anaesthetic (rare).
- **Inhalation injury** with stridor or hypoxia – chemical fumes (ammonia, chlorine) can cause airway edema.
